coredata.dc:title: G-quadruplex formation enhancing energy transfer in self-assembled multilayers and fluorescence recognize for Pb2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xiangying
Peng, Yan
Lin, Youlan
Cai, Lifen
Li, Fang
Liu, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Here we demonstrate a novel strategy for the detection of Pb2+ ions and DNA based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer in the self-assembled multilayers (SAMs). Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs were built by employing graphene oxide (GO) as the energy acceptor, glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH) as the energy donor as well as poly(diallydimethylammonium) chloride (PDDA) and G5 (G-rich DNA) strand as the linker. In the presence of a certain amount of Pb2+ ions, G5 DNA could be folded into G-quadruplex, which was confirmed by Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) experiments. The formation of G-quadruplex could lead to a change in the chain length and the shortening of the distance between GO and GQDs@GSH, thereby, the efficiency of energy transfer between GQDs@GSH and GO was enhanced with the quenching of fluorescence of GQDs@GSH. Meanwhile, Pb2+ ions could be recognized successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: G-quadruplex
Pb2+ ions
Self-assembled multilayers
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Graphene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Apparatus for fluorescence and absorption measurements
G-quadruplex formation enhancing FRET in SAMs
Effect of foreign substances on the SAMs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs with the addition of Pb2+.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of GO and emission spectrum of GQDs@GSH; (b) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs@GSH with different concentrations of GO.
The assembling characterization for SAMs by QCM with flow rate of 50μL/min.
Changes in frequency (f) and dissipation (D) for the grafting of thiol-terminated single stranded DNA on a gold-coated crystal surface at pH 7.4 with different concentration of Pb2+.
Relationship curves between F0/F with different SAMs in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10mM, pH=7.4) and the concentrations of Pb2+.
Fluorescence spectra of Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs with different concentrations of Pb2+ ions in Tris–HCl buffer solution (10mM, pH=7.4) and its corresponding calibration curve inset.
Relationship curves between F0/F of SAMs assembled with different sequences (40nt) DNA and the concentration of Pb2+.
Summary of DNA (40nt) sequences used in this report).
Effects of substances on fluorescence intensity of Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs with the addition of Pb2+ (Pb2+ concentration was 4.5nM)
Analytical results of Pb2+ in real normal blood samples.

head.ce:title: G-quadruplex formation enhancing energy transfer in self-assembled multilayers and fluorescence recognize for Pb2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we demonstrate a novel strategy for the detection of Pb2+ ions and DNA based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer in the self-assembled multilayers (SAMs). Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs were built by employing graphene oxide (GO) as the energy acceptor, glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH) as the energy donor as well as poly(diallydimethylammonium) chloride (PDDA) and G5 (G-rich DNA) strand as the linker. In the presence of a certain amount of Pb2+ ions, G5 DNA could be folded into G-quadruplex, which was confirmed by Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) experiments. The formation of G-quadruplex could lead to a change in the chain length and the shortening of the distance between GO and GQDs@GSH, thereby, the efficiency of energy transfer between GQDs@GSH and GO was enhanced with the quenching of fluorescence of GQDs@GSH. Meanwhile, Pb2+ ions could be recognized successfully.

ce:section.ce:para: The modern medical research reveals that most of serious diseases are considered to be related to gene mutation. Therefore, it is important to detect the specific sequences of DNA for the applications in medical diagnosis, gene analysis and environmental detection and so on. There are a large number of guanine-rich (G-rich) sequences observed in the human genome [1–3], which have significant applications in treating diseases. Since the G-rich sequences can easily form four strand DNA spiral structure (G-quadruplex) in the presence of some cations [4], such as Pb2+ ions, different kinds of sensors have been developed based on Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) in terms of G-quadruplex formation [5–9].

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer that the excited-state donor can transfer its energy to a proximal ground-state acceptor after light excitation, has become a powerful technique to investigate molecular-level changes indicated by the dramatic change of FRET efficiency due to variations in the donor-acceptor distance. Since the size of biomacromolecules match with the distance of FRET occurring, FRET has been applied to detect intracellular biological proteins and nuclear acids or investigate the molecular interactions [10,11].

Self-assembled multilayers (SAMs), possessing the advantages of high sensitivity, simple operation, controllable distance, flexible design, easy to tune the components and repeated use, have been applied in many fields. SAMs combined with fluorescence sensing can provide extremely high sensitivity and selectivity, compared with those in traditional solution cases [12,13]. Furthermore, our research group also combined SAMs with FRET for the first time, reported the correlation of DNA chain length and the energy transfer in self-assembled multilayers [14]. The results indicated that DNA hybridization could change the distance between donor and acceptor as well as affect the energy transfer in self-assembled films.

Herein, we proposed a novel fluorescent SAMs to study the effect of G-quadruplex formation on the FRET in SAMs, in which graphene two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials would be used in FRET assays [15,16]. Graphene oxide (GO) with excellent properties [17,18] is also known to its super quenching capability to fluorophores [19,20], thus chosen as an energy acceptor. Glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH) was used as an energy donor instead of traditional organic dyes, because of its low toxicity, high stability, excellent solubility, stable photoluminescence and good biocompatibility [21]. More importantly, GQDs@GSH fluorescence could not be quenched by some metal ions, such as Pb2+ ions, within a certain range of concentration [22]. We built Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs by employing G5 (G-rich DNA) and poly(diallydimethylammonium) chloride (PDDA) as the bridge. After adding Pb2+ ions with different concentrations, G5 in SAMs could be changed into G-quadruplexes, as shown in Scheme 1 , which was confirmed by quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) experiments. G-quadruplex formation could lead to a change in the chain length and the shortening of the distance between GO and GQDs@GSH. Therefore, the energy transfer was enhanced and the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH was quenched by GO in SAMs. The recognition of guanine-rich DNA and the detection of Pb2+ ions were thereby achieved.

GQDs@GSH [22] and GO [23] were synthesized according to the referred literatures. All oligonucleotides were purchased from invitrogen (Guangzhou, China) and dissolved in 10mM Tris–HCl of pH 7.4, the sequences were listed in Table 1 . Poly(diallydimethylammonium) chloride (PDDA, Mw 100,000–200,000) was obtained from J&K Chemical (Shanghai, China). Other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water purified by Milli-Q system (Millipore, 18.2MΩcm). The quartz substrates were purchased from Jinzhou Jinke Quartz Co., Ltd.

The quartz substrates used for layer-by-layer deposition were ultrasonically cleaned by absolute ethyl alcohol and ultrapure water for 20min, respectively, and then immersed into fresh piranha solution (7:3 (v/v) mixture of 30% H2O2 and 98% H2SO4) for 20min. After that, the substrates were washed by water and dried by high purity nitrogen. The pretreatment substrates were immersed into 0.12g/L GO solution for 1.5h, and then were successively dipped into PDDA (1%,V/V), G5-DNA (1.0μM) and PDDA (1%,V/V) for 2h. At last, the preceding substrates were immersed into GQD@GSH for 2h. Each step for the preparation of monolayers, SAMs were washed with water and dried with nitrogen flow. All SAMs were measured in 10mM Tris–HCl solution of pH 7.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparatus for fluorescence and absorption measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra were measured by a HITACHI F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra of GO and GQDs@GSH solutions were measured by using the SHIMADZU UV-2600 spectrophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra of SAMs were measured with Agilent Cary Series UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer.

All on-line detections were implemented on a Q-Sense E4 QCM-D instrument (Q-Sense AB, Västra Frölunda, Sweden), and the AT-cut quartz crystal with a fundamental resonant frequency of 5MHz and crystal constant (C) of 17.7ng/cm2 Hz were obtained from Q-sense AB. And the results were obtained under the measurements of frequency changes in the third overtone (n =3). The fifth and seventh overtones were not presented, because they exhibited the same trend as that for the third overtone. All the experiments were performed at 25°C, the flow rate was 50μL/min. To remove the physical adsorptions on the surface of the chip, we rinse the substances with ultrapure water between layers.

If there is large overlap between the emission spectrum of donor and the absorption spectrum of acceptor, the acceptor can absorb energy from the donor within close proximity and FRET would happen [24]. GQDs@GSH with the maximum emission at 447nm and GO were chosen as the donor and the acceptor, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the emission spectrum of GQDs@GSH overlaps with the absorption spectrum of GO. According to Fig. 1b, with adding a certain amount of GO, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSHdecreased quickly, which could support the fact that GO can effectively quench the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH. With this in mind, FRET process could occur when GQDs@GSH and GO were employed to act as the donor and the acceptor, respectively.

Thus, Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs was constructed, with GQDs@GSH as the donor, GO as the acceptor, G5(5′-(G3T)4ACCGAGAC(G3T)4) with guanine-rich DNA sequences as the linker between the donor and the acceptor. The assembling of each layer in SAMs was characterized by QCM. The frequency decreasing clearly indicate that increasing the layer mass, the polyelectrolyte chains, G5-DNA and graphene gradually deposit onto the resonator surface driven by the electrostatic interaction, as shown on Fig. 2 .
Then, the influences on the formation of G-quadruplex on the FRET in SAMs were systematically studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: G-quadruplex formation enhancing FRET in SAMs
ce:section.ce:para: Since G5-DNA (length about 16.4nm) could fold into four-stranded structures induced by Pb2+, therefore, for the Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs, when DNA was induced by Pb2+ ions to form G-quadruplex, the distance between GQDs@GSH and GO could be sharply shortened, because the length of four-stranded structures including the loops is about 1.5nm [29]. Then, the FRET from GQDs@GSH to GO in SAMs would be enhanced, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH would decrease, which was confirmed by the following experiments.

When Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs (SAMs 1) was immersed into 1mL 10mM Tris–HCl buffer solution of pH 7.4, with adding Pb2+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of SAMs was found to decrease. Then, other two kinds of SAMs for comparison were prepared. One was Quartz/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs (SAMs 2) without the acceptor of GO, and another was Quartz/GO/PDDA/PSS/PDDA/GQDs@GSH (SAMs 3) without G5. With increasing the concentration of Pb2+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of SAMs 2 or SAMs 3 was found to be unchanged. Fig. 4 shows the relationship curves between relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of three kinds of SAMs and concentration of Pb2+ ions, where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of SAMs at 442 nm in the absence and presence of Pb2+ ions, respectively. It was found that with increasing Pb2+ ions concentration, the F0/F of SAMs 1 increased, and the F0/F of SAMs 2 and SAMs 3 were obviously unchanged.

G5 in SAMs 1 could be folded into G-quadruplexes with increasing Pb2+ ions concentration, thereby the distance between the donor GQDs@GSH and the acceptor GO in SAMs 1 was shorten. The distance change could lead to that, the donor energy of GQDs@GSH was transferred to the acceptor of GO, thus the fluorescence intensity of SAMs 1 decreased. For the controlling experiments, the FRET could not occur in SAMs 2 since there was no GO as the energy accepter, thus no changes of the fluorescence intensity in SAMs 2 could be observed. Clearly, G5 could serve as the linker in SAMs 1, the distance between GQDs@GSH and GO was shorten in the presence of Pb2+ ions, and the energy transfer occurred. However, for SAMs 3 without G5, there was no change of the distance between GQDs@GSH and GO after adding Pb2+ ions, thus there was no changes in efficiency of energy transfer, resulting in no changes of fluorescence intensity by adding Pb2+ ions in SAMs 3. Therefore, the novel Pb2+ ions sensing system based on FRET was expected to be established.

Under the optimal condition, Pb2+ ions with different concentration were added into Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs system, resulted in decreasing fluorescence intensity of SAMs in 10mM Tris–HCl buffer solution pH of 7.4, and the results was shown in Fig. 5 . The inset of Fig. 5 was the variation of F0/F as a function of different concentrations of Pb2+ ions. There was a linear relationship between F0/F and the concentrations of Pb2+ ions varying from 2.4nM to 11.5nM with the low detection limit of 2.2nM.

In order to study the specificity of Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs for G-quadruplexes, we constructed the SAMs with normal 40nt DNA sequences (ssDNA1, ssDNA2, ssDNA3, ssDNA4) (Table 1) instead of G5, the fluorescence intensity of SAMs by adding various concentrations of Pb2+ ions was investigated. Fig. 6 showed the relationship curves of relative fluorescence intensity F0/F of the SAMs with 40nt normal DNA sequences and different concentrations of Pb2+ ions. It was found that, when G5 was replaced by other normal DNA sequences without G-rich, after increasing concentrations of Pb2+ ions, the F0/F of the SAMs was kept unchanged. Therefore, G5 DNA with G-rich could be distinguished.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of foreign substances on the SAMs
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of metal ions and other substances on Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs were studied. The results are shown in Table 2 . Because the following blood serum samples need be diluted 100 times with Tris–HCl buffer (10mM, pH=7.4) for use, the relatively high concentrations of ions and correlation substances in serum samples have little interference for the determination. Thus, the SAMs have good selectivity to Pb2+ ions.

The optimized method was applied for the determination of Pb2+ ions in real normal blood samples. We took the two copies of normal blood serum samples (provided by the hospital of Huaqiao University), which were diluted 100 times with Tris–HCl buffer (10mM, pH=7.4) before use. The SAMs sensor was immersed into blood serum samples, the fluorescence intensity decreased at once and then reached a plateau after 50min. And then we started to determine with the stable SAMs. The lead content in blood samples was determined by spiking recovery experiments. The results given in Table 3 were indicative of the favorable reproducibility, recovery and precision of this method.

In summary, a novel functional thin film (Quartz/GO/PDDA/G5/PDDA/GQDs@GSH SAMs) was designed and prepared by employing GQDs@GSH as the energy donor, GO as the energy acceptor, PDDA and G5-DNA as the linkers. G-quadruplex formation induced by Pb2+ ions enhancing energy transfer in SAMs was explored in the first time. Based on this novel FRET sensing assay, G-rich DNA could be distinguished and the detection concentration of Pb2+ ions was lowered to be 2.2nM. Furthermore, the results show that the sensor has the advantages including simple operation and great selectivity for Pb2+ ions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21275059, 21377124 and 21575044), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (Grant nos. 2015J01054 and 2016J01062), the Project of New Faculty of Huaqiao University (Grant no. 15BS202) and Promotion Program for Young and Middle-aged Teacher in Science and Technology Research of Huaqiao University (Grant no. ZQN-YX303).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Sun received her B.Sc. degree in 1982 and M.Sc. degree in 1989 in Analytical Chemistry from East China Nomal University. Her D.Sc. in Analytical Chemistry was awarded in 2003 from Xiamen University. In 1991, she started his study on analytical chemistry at Huaqiao University as a teacher. She is currently a professor of Huaqiao University. Her research is focused on (1) Constructing of novel fluorescence sensor based on self-assembled multilayers; (2) Developing nanosensor for environment and bioanalysis. She is the author and co-author of more than 60 original papers published in international journals.
